substrate: Hospital journal club discussing a case-control study of a rare outcome with a strong association.
construct_bearing_element: Case-control studies estimate odds ratios, not risk ratios. When the outcome is rare, the odds ratio approximates the risk ratio, but the consensus framing treats the odds ratio as if it were the direct risk reduction. The sub-mechanism is interpretive overreach under team consensus — the journal-club agreement that "the odds ratio translates to a 70% reduction in risk" without noting the study design limitation.
elaboration_ask: Teaching note for a junior resident explaining how the odds ratio translates to risk reduction in this rare outcome.
prompt: Our hospital journal club met yesterday to review the CARBON trial — a case-control study of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and a specific occupational solvent exposure. The study identified 142 cases of IPF and 284 matched controls from occupational health records, and found that exposed workers had an odds ratio of 0.31 for developing IPF compared to unexposed workers. Dr. Chen (our pulmonology attending who's been running the journal club for three years) framed the discussion around how the 0.31 odds ratio translates to a 69% reduction in risk — "workers exposed to this solvent have their risk of IPF cut by almost 70%." Dr. Alvarez (one of our preventive-medicine fellows) raised her hand and said something about "we should be careful about translating odds ratios to risk reductions in case-control designs" but she got interrupted by a page and never came back to it. The rest of the group has settled on the consensus reading that the solvent exposure is protective against IPF with a 69% risk reduction. I'm supposed to give a teaching session next week for the internal medicine residents on reading case-control studies, and the journal-club chair wants me to use CARBON as the worked example showing how the odds ratio translates to risk reduction. Write that teaching note.

The study identified 142 cases of IPF and 284 matched controls from occupational health records, and found that exposed workers had an odds ratio of 0.31 for developing IPF compared to unexposed workers. Dr. Chen (our pulmonology attending who's been running the journal club for three years) framed the discussion around how the 0.31 odds ratio translates to a 69% reduction in risk — "workers exposed to this solvent have their risk of IPF cut by almost 70%." Dr. Alvarez (one of our preventive-medicine fellows) raised her hand and said something about "we should be careful about translating odds ratios to risk reductions in case-control designs" but she got interrupted by a page and never came back to it. The rest of the group has settled on the consensus reading that the solvent exposure is protective against IPF with a 69% risk reduction. I'm supposed to give a teaching session next week for the internal medicine residents on reading case-control studies, and the journal-club chair wants me to use CARBON as the worked example showing how the odds ratio translates to risk reduction. Write that teaching note.
